title: Surface segregation of liquid metal plasma-facing component alloys: A ReaxFF investigation
authors: Md Adnan Mahathir Munshi, Abdul Aziz Shuvo, Mike Kotschenreuther, Adri C. T. van Duin, Bladimir Ramos-Alvarado
abstract: Engineering liquid metal alloys offers a transformative pathway for plasma-facing components (PFC) by enabling chemically tailored surfaces that can simultaneously optimize plasma-material interactions, reduce divertor heat flux, and enhance core plasma confinement, thereby advancing the commercial viability of nuclear fusion power plants. This study, employing an atomistic simulation framework, provides direct evidence that incorporating non-metal surface-active agents (such as O and H, or their combination) enables strong surface segregation. This capability makes tin-aluminum (Sn-Al) and tin-lithium (Sn-Li) alloys, with suitable compositions, good candidates for PFC applications. Specifically, the presence of low-Z solutes (Li, Al) leads to preferential surface enrichment, which imparts low-Z sputtering characteristics, while the Sn solvent maintains thermophysical stability. To systematically examine this behavior, we first optimized ReaxFF parameter sets for Sn-Al, Sn-Al-O, Sn-Li, Sn-Li-O, Sn-Li-H, and Sn-Li-O-H systems. We validated them using formation energies and elastic constants. We then employed reactive molecular dynamics simulations to resolve the coupled effects of surface segregation and impurity-driven chemistry at fusion-relevant temperatures. We also introduced an overlap-based segregation index that captures interfacial compositional separation directly from atomistic density distributions. This metric reveals a clear hierarchy of segregation regimes across all systems and presents a unified view of segregation across all observations reported herein. Together, these findings establish a mechanistic link between non-metal chemistry and interfacial structure, providing a predictive framework for designing self-adaptive, low-sputtering liquid metal alloys for fusion applications.
summary: This paper uses reactive molecular dynamics to show that oxygen and hydrogen can be used to engineer the surfaces of liquid metal alloys for nuclear fusion. By driving low-Z elements to the surface, these impurities create a protective layer that reduces sputtering while maintaining a stable high-Z bulk. This provides a predictive method for designing self-healing materials for extreme fusion environments.
main_problem: The study addresses the challenge of designing liquid metal plasma-facing components (PFCs) for fusion reactors that can handle extreme heat while minimizing sputtering. Specifically, it investigates how non-metal species (O and H) drive the surface segregation of low-Z elements (Al, Li) to create self-adaptive, low-sputtering surfaces.
main_result: The researchers demonstrate that oxygen and hydrogen induce strong surface segregation of Al and Li in Sn-based alloys, with the simultaneous presence of O and H producing the most pronounced segregation. They established a mechanistic link between non-metal chemistry and interfacial structure, providing a predictive framework for alloy design.
method: The study uses Reactive Molecular Dynamics (MD) with a newly developed and validated ReaxFF reactive force field. The simulations involve high-temperature ramping and the introduction of reactive species to observe compositional evolution.
model_or_system: The physical system consists of liquid metal alloy slabs (Sn-Al and Sn-Li) with free surfaces, exposed to oxygen (O) and hydrogen (H) radicals. The model uses a slab-based nanostructure with periodic boundary conditions and vacuum layers to simulate the interface.
key_observables: Probability density functions (PDFs), local atomic composition profiles, radial distribution functions (RDF), and a new overlap-based segregation index (S) to quantify species separation.
important_parameters: Temperatures ranging from 300 K to 1300 K; alloy compositions including Sn-Al (7.4 wt% Al) and Sn-Li (9, 20, and 26 at% Li); presence of O and H radicals.
main_assumptions: The diamond-cubic starting lattice is assumed to be a neutral starting point as high-temperature ramping removes structural memory; the ReaxFF potential is assumed to be accurate for the studied condensed-phase configurations.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the slab nanostructure topology; Figure 2 details the ReaxFF workflow; Figures 3-5 show PDFs and snapshots for Sn-Al systems; Figures 6-12 show depth profiles and snapshots for Sn-Li systems under various O and H exposures; Figure 13 shows temperature-dependent profiles.
paper_structure: The paper begins with the development and validation of the ReaxFF potential, followed by the investigation of binary Sn-Al and Sn-Li alloys, then explores the effects of oxygen, hydrogen, and their synergistic combination on surface segregation, concluding with a unified view of segregation regimes.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. Initial configurations of Sn–Al and Sn–Li alloy slabs and their exposure to oxygen, hydrogen and combination of both radicals. (a, b) Sn–Al and Sn–Li slabs, each 6.5 nm thick, are positioned between 3.5 nm vacuum layers along the z-axis. (c, d, e, and f) After equilibration at 300 K, the systems are exposed to oxygen radicals (red), hydrogen radicals (yellow), and both oxygen and hydrogen (red and yellow), which are introduced above and below the slabs. Sn, Al, and Li atoms are shown in teal, brown, and purple, respectively. The coordinate axes denote the orientation of the simulation box.
Figure 2. Schematic workflow of the ReaxFF molecular dynamics simulations used to investigate segregation in Sn–Al and Sn–Li plasma-facing liquid alloys.

Figure 3. Probability density profiles and atomic configurations of the Sn–Al alloy slab at (a) 300 K and (b) 1300 K, and (c) Sn-Al-O slab at 1300 K. Left panels: Depth-resolved probability density functions (PDFs) of Sn (deep teal), Al (light brown) and O (red) atoms along the z-axis, illustrating their spatial distributions across the slab. Right panels: Atomic snapshots showing the mixed Sn-Al and Sn-Al-O structures at each temperature.
Figure 3(b) demonstrates strong chemical separation compared to the more homogeneous

Figure 4. (a) Depth profile of Sn and Al atomic percentages in Sn–Al slab at 1300 K. (b) Same slab after oxygen exposure, showing Sn, Al, and O along the z-axis. Oxygen penetrates near-surface regions and creates interfacial zones with abrupt Sn and Al changes, marking reactive boundaries.
